figures.caption: Fig. 12. When the input OF depicts very thin shapes, using our default method and default initial scaffold may fail to capture them even at fine mesh resolution (a,b); this can be mitigated by using a significantly higher number of refinement iterations (c) or a finer initial scaffold (d). Box delineates the OF domain.
Fig. 9. Optimizing our iso-surface meshes by collapsing short edges and flipping edges to improve smoothness and aspect ratio: (ac) initial iso-surface meshes. (bd) Optimized meshes.
Fig. 10. Curvature adaptive sampling: (ac) Standard ADS outputs; (bd) Strongly curvature adapted outputs with more samples in higher curvature areas.

Table 1. Quantitative comparison of ADS (bottom) against prior art (random ray stabbing (RRS) [Cline et al. 2009], uniform ray casting (IUS) [Ling et al. 2025], marching cubes (MC) [Lorensen and Cline 1987], and occupancy dual contouring (ODC) [Hwang and Sung 2024]) across different resolutions (low, medium, high, 𝜏 = 0.05, 0.03, 0.02 respectively) and datasets. ADS consistently achieves better accuracy (lower chamfer) and generates more samples while requiring less function evaluations. It is faster than RRS, IUS and ODC and becomes faster than MC at higher sampling resolution.

Fig. 8. A gallery of our samplings and isosurface meshes using different sampling resolutions and input sources. Skateboard and motorcycle [Zhang et al. 2023], spiral, Sapphos, happy Buddha [Zhang et al. 2024], Lucy, dragon, vaselion [Barill et al. 2018].

Fig. 7. Additional visual comparisons of our ADS method to uniform ray casting [Ling et al. 2025], marching cubes (MC) [Lorensen and Cline 1987], and occupancy dual contouring (ODC) [Hwang and Sung 2024]. We achieve higher accuracy and higher sample count with fewer occupancy function evaluations and less time. Contrary to [Ling et al. 2025], we generate both samples and iso-surfaces. Contrary to grid-based methods, we produce random, unbiased samplings.

Fig. 4. ADS overview (in 2D). Left to right: (a) we sample a coarse, well-spaced initial point set within the bounding domain using Poisson disc sampling, then construct its Delaunay triangulation. (b) We iteratively refine the scaffold by identifying crossing edges, and inserting new vertices along them. (c) The surface is extracted using marching tetrahedra, and locally refined to capture thin or high-curvature iso-surface details; (d) the final output iso-surface and samples.
